# Joshua 9: The Price of Neglecting God's Counsel

The news of Israel's victories at Jericho and Ai has spread fear throughout Canaan. Every king west of the Jordan prepares for war, understanding that Joshua and his people represent an unstoppable force. But the Gibeonites choose a different strategy. Rather than fight, they deceive. They construct an elaborate lie—worn cloaks, cracked wineskins, moldy bread, patched sandals—all designed to convince Israel that they come from a distant land beyond the reach of God's promise to destroy the Canaanites. The deception is brilliant, carefully plotted, and almost completely successful. The account does not hide what happened next: "the men of Israel sampled their provisions but did not seek the counsel of the LORD." There it is. The tragic turning point is not the Gibeonites' falsehood but Israel's failure to ask God. Joshua, who has just witnessed the supernatural collapse of Jericho's walls and the miraculous victory at Ai, makes a binding treaty without consulting the One who has guided every step.

What makes this moment cut so deeply is how believable the Gibeonites make their story. They speak the right language—invoking God's fame, recounting His deeds in Egypt, acknowledging His power over the Amorite kings. They position themselves as servants, humbling themselves before Israel. They explain their journey in terms that fit the false narrative perfectly. The test that should have exposed them—examining provisions to verify their claim—actually reinforces the lie. They have thought through every detail. Yet the fundamental failure lies not with their cunning but with Israel's spiritual carelessness. In a moment when they have every reason to pray, they do not. They evaluate the evidence with their eyes and minds alone, forgetting that the God who dried up the Jordan and flattened Jericho's walls is still present and still speaking to those who seek Him.

The consequences unfold with painful precision. Three days after the treaty, the truth emerges. The Gibeonites are not distant travelers but neighbors—dwellers in the very land God promised to clear before Israel. The congregation's immediate instinct is to break the oath and destroy them, but the leaders face an impossible bind: they have sworn "by the LORD, the God of Israel." To violate that oath would bring God's wrath upon them, even though the oath itself was made in ignorance and deception. They cannot undo what they have done. So they do what they can—they honor the oath and reduce the Gibeonites to perpetual servitude. It is a resolution that preserves the integrity of their word while limiting the damage, but there is no escaping the cost of their earlier neglect. The Gibeonites live because Israel bound themselves to let them live.

What Joshua 9 lays bare is a truth that transcends this moment in history: spiritual carelessness always has consequences, and no amount of practical wisdom can substitute for seeking God's will. The irony cuts deep—Israel had already learned that God fights for them in ways no human strategy can match. They had seen the impossible made plain. Yet when a subtle test arrived, dressed in humble clothing and speaking flattering words about God, they defaulted to what they could see and touch. They did not ask. And a people called to be a light to the nations—set apart by God to inherit the land through obedience—found themselves bound to the very people they were meant to displace, all because they failed to pause and seek the counsel of the One who had never failed them.
